title: Label-free biochemical imaging and timepoint analysis of neural organoids via deep learning-enhanced Raman microspectroscopy
text: Bhatt et al. (2025) present a non-invasive, label-free imaging platform that integrates Raman microspectroscopy with deep learning-based hyperspectral unmixing for unsupervised, spatially resolved biochemical analysis of neural organoids. The approach enables high-resolution mapping of cellular and subcellular structures in both cryosectioned and intact 3D organoids, achieving improved imaging accuracy and robustness compared to conventional hyperspectral analysis methods. A key demonstration is volumetric imaging of a neural rosette within a neural organoid. Timepoint analysis revealed spatiotemporal variations in lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids during early neural organoid developmental stages. The platform is non-invasive and requires no exogenous labelling, overcoming limitations of standard imaging techniques. This work builds on the Stevens Lab's prior development of physics-constrained autoencoder-based unmixing (Georgiev/Weier et al. 2024, PNAS) and the RamanSPy framework (Georgiev et al. 2024, Anal. Chem.), extending them to 3D organoid models with broad implications for organoid phenotyping and drug screening.
authors: Bhatt et al. (including Molly M. Stevens and collaborators; X. Zhao affiliated with Supervision Medicine noted in competing interests; full author list not fully parseable from preprint landing page)

title: Label-free biochemical imaging and timepoint analysis of neural organoids via deep learning-enhanced Raman microspectroscopy — Methods & Technical Details
text: Platform: Raman microspectroscopy integrated with deep learning-based hyperspectral unmixing (unsupervised). Samples: cryosectioned and intact neural organoids. Imaging mode: volumetric (3D) and 2D cross-sections. Key biomolecular signals tracked: lipids, proteins, nucleic acids. Temporal analysis: early developmental stage timepoints compared. Architecture: builds on Stevens Lab RamanSPy/unmixing autoencoder framework (Georgiev et al. 2024, Weier/Georgiev et al. 2024).

title: Label-free biochemical imaging and timepoint analysis of neural organoids via deep learning-enhanced Raman microspectroscopy — Key Findings
text: 1. First demonstration of non-invasive, label-free Raman microspectroscopy combined with deep learning hyperspectral unmixing for neural organoid biochemical imaging. 2. Platform enables high-resolution mapping of cellular and subcellular structures in both cryosectioned and intact 3D organoids. 3. Improved imaging accuracy and robustness versus conventional hyperspectral analysis methods. 4. Demonstrated volumetric 3D imaging of a neural rosette within a neural organoid. 5. Revealed spatiotemporal variations in lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids during early organoid developmental stages. 6. Enables unsupervised (label-free, no exogenous dyes) biochemical phenotyping across timepoints.

title: Hyperspectral unmixing for Raman spectroscopy via physics-constrained autoencoders
text: Georgiev et al. (2024, PNAS) developed four autoencoder (AE) neural network architectures for hyperspectral unmixing of Raman spectroscopy data: Dense, Deep Dense (5 hidden layers), Convolutional, and Transformer-based, all with physics-constrained non-negative linear or bilinear decoders enforcing Abundance Non-negativity and Sum-to-one constraints. In systematic validation across 1,650 synthetic benchmark experiments (10,000 spectra/dataset, 100×100 pixel scenes, spectral range 400–1,800 cm⁻¹), AEs outperformed conventional N-FINDR+FCLS and VCA+FCLS on all linear and bilinear mixture scenarios using SAD and MSE metrics. Experimental validation used glucose, sucrose, fructose, and maltose sugar mixtures (1 mol/L; 64 mixtures; 96-well plate; 36.3 mW; 0.5 or 5 s integration; Leica 10×/0.25 NA), showing AEs substantially outperform conventional methods when pure pixels are absent, both at high and low SNR. On volumetric THP-1 monocytic cell Raman imaging data (10 z-slices, 0.3 s integration), AEs recovered cleaner biochemical signatures and uniquely detected cholesterol (peaks: 1,069/1,134/1,300/1,443 cm⁻¹) missed by VCA+NNLS, alongside PBS buffer (1,637 cm⁻¹), cytoplasm (1,005/1,250/1,659/1,445 cm⁻¹), TAGs/PLPs (1,092/1,308/1,445/1,661 cm⁻¹), and nucleus/DNA (790/1,103 cm⁻¹). All AEs were computationally faster than conventional methods (CPU-only, MacBook Air M2). The method is implemented in RamanSPy (v0.3.0+) and data deposited at Zenodo 10779223.
authors: Dimitar Georgiev, Álvaro Fernández-Galiana, Simon Vilms Pedersen, Georgios Papadopoulos, Ruoxiao Xie, Molly M. Stevens, Mauricio Barahona
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS)

title: Hyperspectral unmixing for Raman spectroscopy via physics-constrained autoencoders — Methods & Technical Details
text: Architectures: Dense AE (2 FC layers D→256→K softmax); Deep Dense AE (5 layers 256→128→64→32→16→K); Convolutional AE (2×1D Conv 16 filters size 3/5 + FC); Transformer AE (FC 128 + 2-head attention h=64 + 2 FC + layer norm/dropout 0.1). Decoders: linear (non-negative weights) or bilinear Fan model. Constraints: Abundance Non-negativity (ANC) + Abundance Sum-to-one (ASC) via softmax. Training: 200 (synthetic) or 500–1,000 (experimental) epochs; Adam optimizer LR=0.001; SAD loss (+ MSE for experimental with α=0.1). Synthetic datasets: 10,000 spectra per dataset, 4 scenarios (ideal, +artifacts, +realistic, +bilinear), 3 abundance scenes (Chessboard, Gaussian, Dirichlet), 5 replicates × 5 initializations = 25 per variant, 1,650 total experiments. Spectral range: 400–1,800 cm⁻¹, N=1,401 bands. Experimental benchmark: glucose/sucrose/fructose/maltose at 1 mol/L; 64 mixtures + 5 pure refs; 375 µL/well 96-well plates; high SNR (1,920 spectra, 5 s, 36.3 mW) and low SNR (7,680 spectra, 0.5 s, 36.3 mW); Leica 10×/0.25 NA objective; spectral range 142–3,684.8 cm⁻¹. Biological: THP-1 volumetric scan (0.3 s integration, 10 z-slices; zenodo:256329 scan '001'); preprocessing: crop 700–1,800 cm⁻¹, cosmic removal, Savitzky-Golay (cubic, 21), AsLS baseline (λ=10⁵, p=0.001), MinMax [0,1]; K=6 endmembers, 1,000 epochs, ANC only. Software: RamanSPy v0.3.0+; GitHub: raman-unmixing-aes; Dataset: Zenodo 10779223.

title: Hyperspectral unmixing for Raman spectroscopy via physics-constrained autoencoders — Key Findings
text: 1. Four autoencoder (AE) architectures developed for Raman hyperspectral unmixing: Dense, Deep Dense (5 hidden layers: 256→128→64→32→16→K), Convolutional (1D Conv), and Transformer-based. 2. Linear and bilinear (Fan model) decoders enable physics-constrained unmixing. 3. On 12 synthetic benchmark scenarios (1,650 total experiments, 10,000 spectra each, 100×100 pixel scenes), AEs outperform N-FINDR+FCLS and VCA+FCLS on all linear and bilinear variants (better SAD and MSE). 4. On sugar mixture experimental data (glucose, sucrose, fructose, maltose; 64 mixtures; 96-well plates; high and low SNR conditions at 36.3 mW, 0.5–5 s integration), AEs substantially outperform conventional methods especially when pure pixels are absent. 5. On volumetric THP-1 monocytic cell data, AEs recover cleaner biochemical signatures and detect cholesterol (missed by VCA+NNLS); identified endmembers include PBS buffer (1,637 cm⁻¹), cytoplasm (1,005/1,250/1,659/1,445 cm⁻¹), TAGs/PLPs (1,092/1,308/1,445/1,661 cm⁻¹), nucleus/DNA (790/1,103 cm⁻¹), cholesterol (1,069/1,134/1,300/1,443 cm⁻¹). 6. Computational efficiency: all AEs faster than conventional methods (CPU-only, MacBook Air M2).

title: RamanSPy: An Open-Source Python Package for Integrative Raman Spectroscopy Data Analysis
text: Georgiev et al. (2024, Anal. Chem. 96, 8492–8500) introduced RamanSPy, an open-source Python package addressing fragmentation and lack of reproducibility in Raman spectroscopic analysis by providing a comprehensive, modular library for preprocessing, unmixing, machine learning integration, and synthetic data generation. Preprocessing includes spectral cropping (700–1800 or 500–1800 cm⁻¹), cosmic ray removal (Whitaker & Hayes), Savitzky-Golay denoising (polynomial order 3, kernel 7, Gaussian noise σ=0.15), asymmetric least squares baseline correction (α=500), and MinMax normalization. Spectral unmixing via N-FINDR (5 endmembers) and FCLS on THP-1 cell data identified cytoplasm (1,008 cm⁻¹), nucleic acid (789 cm⁻¹), and lipids (1,066/1,134/1,303/1,443/1,747 cm⁻¹). Pipeline I processing is highly efficient: 0.25 s (1,000 spectra), 2.5 s (10,000 spectra), 20–25 s (100,000 spectra) on MacBook Air M2. A pretrained 1D ResUNet deep-learning denoiser outperformed 6 Savitzky-Golay variants on low-SNR Raman spectra. For AI-based bacteria identification across 30 isolates, logistic regression yielded 79.63% species-level and 94.63% antibiotic treatment classification accuracy (100 spectra/isolate, 28-model benchmark). RamanSPy integrates with scikit-learn, PyTorch, and tensorflow and supports datasets including bacterial species, cell lines, COVID-19, SERS, wheat, and minerals.
authors: Dimitar Georgiev, Simon Vilms Pedersen, Ruoxiao Xie, Álvaro Fernández-Galiana, Molly M. Stevens, Mauricio Barahona

title: RamanSPy: An Open-Source Python Package for Integrative Raman Spectroscopy Data Analysis — Methods & Technical Details
text: Published in Anal. Chem. 2024, 96, 21, 8492–8500. Preprocessing pipelines: spectral cropping (700–1800 cm⁻¹ or 500–1800 cm⁻¹), cosmic ray removal (Whitaker & Hayes algorithm), denoising (Savitzky-Golay polynomial order 3 kernel size 7; Gaussian filter), baseline correction (asymmetric least squares; 'shape' method α=500), normalization (Global MinMax [0,1]; pixel-wise AUC=1). Unmixing: N-FINDR (5 endmembers), FCLS. Deep-learning denoiser: 1D ResUNet pretrained on MDA-MB-231 data; Gaussian noise σ=0.15 added for low-SNR test. ML: scikit-learn, PyTorch, tensorflow; lazypredict benchmarks 28 models. Synthetic data: 1,500 uniform [0,1) spectra; sizes 1,000/10,000/100,000. Datasets supported: bacterial species (Ho et al. 2019), cell lines (Kallepitis et al. 2017; Horgan et al. 2021), COVID-19, SERS adenine, wheat lines, minerals, synthetic Raman generator. Spectral peak assignments: cytoplasm 1008 cm⁻¹, nucleic acid 789 cm⁻¹, lipids 1066/1134/1303/1443/1747 cm⁻¹. Hardware: MacBook Air Apple M2 chip, 8-core CPU, 10-core GPU, 16-core Neural Engine. Repository: https://github.com/barahona-research-group/RamanSPy. Documentation: https://ramanspy.readthedocs.io.

title: RamanSPy: An Open-Source Python Package for Integrative Raman Spectroscopy Data Analysis — Key Findings
text: 1. RamanSPy is an open-source Python package providing a comprehensive, modular library for Raman spectroscopic analysis—preprocessing, unmixing, ML integration, synthetic data generation. 2. Preprocessing benchmarks: three representative protocols on THP-1 cell data yielded substantially different results, reinforcing the importance of transparency/reproducibility. 3. Spectral unmixing (N-FINDR, 5 endmembers + FCLS) on THP-1 data identified: cytoplasm (1008 cm⁻¹), nucleic acid (789 cm⁻¹), lipids (1066, 1134, 1303, 1443, 1747 cm⁻¹), and background. 4. Computational efficiency of Pipeline I: 0.25 s (1,000 spectra), 2.5 s (10,000 spectra), 20–25 s (100,000 spectra) on MacBook Air M2. 5. Deep-learning denoiser (1D ResUNet) outperformed 6 Savitzky-Golay filters on low-SNR test sets (MSE, SAD, SID metrics). 6. AI-based bacteria identification: logistic regression achieved 79.63% species-level accuracy and 94.63% antibiotic treatment classification accuracy (30 bacterial/yeast isolates, 100 spectra/isolate, 28 models benchmarked with lazypredict). 7. 12,829 total article views; 17 citations (at time of data extraction).

title: High-Throughput Molecular Imaging via Deep-Learning-Enabled Raman Spectroscopy
text: Horgan et al. (2021, Anal. Chem. 93, 15850–15860) introduced DeepeR, a deep learning framework for high-throughput Raman molecular imaging. A 1D ResUNet trained on >1.5 million spectra (400 h acquisition, 532 nm, 11 cells, 172,312 spectral pairs) achieved 10× improvement in MSE for spectral denoising over PCA (ResUNet MSE: 2.85×10⁻³; PCA: 2.96×10⁻²). A complementary HyRISR super-resolution network achieved 2–4× spatial super-resolution of hyperspectral Raman images (64×64×500 voxels) with superior PSNR, SSIM, and MSE versus nearest-neighbor and bicubic methods. Combined, DeepeR achieves 40–90× imaging speed-up, enabling cellular imaging in under 1 min, and 160× speed-up for large-area/low-resolution applications. Transfer learning extends the framework from cell to tissue-scale imaging. Key molecular peaks tracked: DNA (795 cm⁻¹), phenylalanine (1,004 cm⁻¹), lipids (1,300/1,440 cm⁻¹), and amide I proteins (1,660 cm⁻¹). Data augmentation used random cropping, flipping, rotation, mixup, and spectral flipping/shifting; 85:10:5 train:val:test split across 169 images.
authors: Conor C. Horgan, Magnus Jensen, Anika Nagelkerke, Jean-Philippe St-Pierre, Tom Vercauteren, Molly M. Stevens, Mads S. Bergholt

title: High-Throughput Molecular Imaging via Deep-Learning-Enabled Raman Spectroscopy — Methods & Technical Details
text: Architecture 1 – Spectral Denoiser: 1D ResUNet trained on MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells (532 nm excitation); inputs with Gaussian noise at low SNR. Architecture 2 – Super-resolution: HyRISR network; 2×, 3×, 4× skip downsampling to generate low-resolution inputs. Data augmentation: random image cropping, flipping, rotation, mixup, spectral flipping/shifting. Image dimensions: 64×64×500 or 63×63×500 (height×width×spectral channels). Metrics: PSNR, SSIM, MSE. Molecular peak assignments: 795 cm⁻¹ (DNA), 1,004 cm⁻¹ (phenylalanine), 1,300/1,440 cm⁻¹ (lipids), 1,660 cm⁻¹ (amide I of proteins). Training dataset: >1.5 million spectra from 400 h of Raman acquisition; n=11 cells with 532 nm laser; n=172,312 spectral pairs; n=169 Raman images; 85:10:5 train:val:test split. Code/models: https://github.com/conor-horgan/. Published Anal. Chem. 2021, 93(48), 15850–15860.

title: High-Throughput Molecular Imaging via Deep-Learning-Enabled Raman Spectroscopy — Key Findings
text: 1. DeepeR framework combines deep-learning denoising (1D ResUNet) and super-resolution (HyRISR) to achieve 40–90× Raman imaging speed-up, enabling cellular imaging in under 1 min. 2. Spectral denoising: 10× improvement in MSE over common Raman filtering methods (ResUNet MSE: 2.85×10⁻³ [95% CI: 2.55–3.15×10⁻³]; PCA MSE: 2.96×10⁻² [95% CI: 2.55–3.38×10⁻²]). 3. HyRISR network achieves 2–4× spatial super-resolution of hyperspectral Raman images (superior to nearest neighbor and bicubic upsampling in PSNR, SSIM, MSE). 4. Combined denoising + super-resolution achieves up to 40–90× speed-up. 5. 160× speed-up demonstrated for lower-resolution applications (large-area screening, spectral pathology). 6. Transfer learning extends DeepeR from cell to tissue-scale imaging. 7. Training dataset: >1.5 million spectra, 400 h acquisition; n=11 cells (532 nm laser), n=172,312 spectral pairs, n=169 Raman images (85:10:5 train:val:test split). 8. Molecular peaks tracked: 795 cm⁻¹ (DNA), 1,004 cm⁻¹ (phenylalanine), 1,300 and 1,440 cm⁻¹ (lipids), 1,660 cm⁻¹ (amide I, proteins). Published in Anal. Chem. 2021, 93, 48, 15850–15860.

title: Auxetic Cardiac Patches with Tunable Mechanical and Conductive Properties toward Treating Myocardial Infarction
text: Kapnisi et al. (2018, Adv. Funct. Mater. DOI: 10.1002/adfm.201800618) introduced the auxetic conductive cardiac patch (AuxCP) for myocardial infarction treatment. The patch is fabricated by excimer laser microablation of a chitosan-polyaniline composite into a re-entrant honeycomb (bow-tie) pattern, producing a negative Poisson's ratio. This auxetic geometry allows the patch to conform to the anisotropic, time-varying mechanics of the beating heart. The bow-tie design imparts a tunable range of mechanical strength and anisotropy, adjustable to match native cardiac tissue properties. The conducting polyaniline component enables electrical interfacing with the electroresponsive myocardium. In vitro studies confirmed cytocompatibility with murine neonatal cardiomyocytes. Ex vivo cardiac electrophysiology studies showed no detrimental effects on healthy or MI rat hearts, and better mechanical conformability compared to unpatterned material. A rat MI model showed no adverse effect on cardiac function and negligible fibrosis at two weeks in vivo, establishing this as a versatile platform for cardiac biomaterial design.
authors: Michaella Kapnisi, Catherine Mansfield, Camille Marijon, Anne Geraldine Guex, Filippo Perbellini, Ifigeneia Bardi, Eleanor J. Humphrey, Jennifer L. Puetzer, Damia Mawad, Demosthenes C. Koutsogeorgis, Daniel J. Stuckey, Cesare M. Terracciano, Sian E. Harding, Molly M. Stevens

title: Auxetic Cardiac Patches with Tunable Mechanical and Conductive Properties toward Treating Myocardial Infarction — Methods & Technical Details
text: Material: chitosan-polyaniline composite. Fabrication method: excimer laser microablation to create re-entrant honeycomb (bow-tie) auxetic pattern. Design: negative Poisson's ratio achieved through bow-tie/re-entrant honeycomb geometry. In vitro: cytocompatibility with murine neonatal cardiomyocytes confirmed. Ex vivo: electrophysiological studies on healthy and MI rat hearts. In vivo: rat MI model; assessment of cardiac function and fibrotic response at 2 weeks post-implant. Keywords: auxetic materials, biomaterials, cardiac patches, doped-conjugated polymers, re-entrant honeycombs.

title: Auxetic Cardiac Patches with Tunable Mechanical and Conductive Properties toward Treating Myocardial Infarction — Key Findings
text: 1. First auxetic conductive cardiac patch (AuxCP) with negative Poisson's ratio enabling mechanical conformability to heart tissue. 2. Re-entrant honeycomb (bow-tie) design micropatterned via excimer laser microablation into chitosan-polyaniline composite. 3. Bow-tie geometry allows wide tunable range of mechanical strength and anisotropy to match native myocardium. 4. Patches are conductive and cytocompatible with murine neonatal cardiomyocytes in vitro. 5. Ex vivo: no detrimental effect on electrophysiology of healthy or MI rat hearts; better conformability to heart movements than unpatterned material. 6. In vivo rat MI model: no detrimental effect on cardiac function and negligible fibrotic response at 2 weeks. 7. Versatile platform for cardiac biomaterial design for MI treatment.

title: Toward Regeneration of the Heart: Bioengineering Strategies for Immunomodulation
text: Ferrini et al. (2019, Front. Cardiovasc. Med. 6:26) reviewed bioengineering strategies combining immunomodulatory therapies with cardiac biomaterials to treat myocardial infarction and prevent subsequent heart failure. A key statistic: an average-sized MI destroys up to 1 billion cardiomyocytes, and over 20% of MI patients develop heart failure—an incurable fibrotic condition. The review identifies injectable hydrogels loaded with bioactive factors (angiogenic, proliferative, anti-apoptotic, and immunomodulatory) delivered minimally invasively as a promising strategy to modulate the post-MI immune cascade, prevent adverse left ventricular remodeling, and limit the progression from acute inflammation to chronic fibrosis. The review covers injectable hydrogel systems and bioactive factors tested in animal models, and discusses translation challenges. This work was funded by the British Heart Foundation (PG/16/93/32345).
authors: Arianna Ferrini, Molly M. Stevens, Susanne Sattler, Nadia Rosenthal

title: Toward Regeneration of the Heart: Bioengineering Strategies for Immunomodulation — Methods & Technical Details
text: Review article (Frontiers Cardiovasc Med 2019 Mar 21; 6:26). Publication type: Review. Funding: British Heart Foundation PG/16/93/32345. Scope: injectable hydrogels as drug delivery vehicles; bioactive factors including growth factors with angiogenic (e.g., VEGF), proliferative, anti-apoptotic, and immunomodulatory functions; immune cascade modulation post-MI; left ventricular remodeling prevention; transition from inflammation to fibrosis. Diseases covered: myocardial infarction, heart failure.

title: Toward Regeneration of the Heart: Bioengineering Strategies for Immunomodulation — Key Findings
text: 1. Comprehensive review of bioengineering strategies combining immunomodulation with cardiac biomaterials for post-MI heart failure treatment. 2. Average-sized MI destroys up to 1 billion cardiomyocytes; >20% of MI patients subsequently develop heart failure. 3. Identified injectable hydrogels loaded with angiogenic, proliferative, anti-apoptotic and immunomodulatory bioactive factors as a key therapeutic strategy. 4. Minimally invasive delivery via injectable hydrogels can prevent adverse left ventricular remodeling and modulate immune cascade from inflammation to fibrosis. 5. Emphasizes synergy between immunomodulatory therapies and cardiac bioengineering as an emerging field. 6. Review covers hydrogel systems and bioactive factors with demonstrated efficacy in animal models.

title: A conducting polymer with enhanced electronic stability applied in cardiac models
text: Hardy et al. (2016, Sci. Adv. DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.1601007) developed a conducting polymer cardiac patch with greatly enhanced electronic stability by growing PANI doped with phytic acid on chitosan film. Phytic acid chelation with chitosan immobilizes the dopant, preventing the typical rapid deterioration seen in conventional CP constructs (e.g., PCL-PANI loses conductivity within 24 h vs. >14 days for the new patch). The patch retains surface resistivity of 35.85 ± 9.40 kΩ/sq and conductivity of 0.162 ± 0.043 S/cm (dry: 7.93 ± 2.64 kΩ/sq) after 2 weeks in physiological PBS, confirmed by XPS retention of ~12% protonated N species (vs. ~6% for PCL-PANI at 7 days). Mechanical properties: Young's modulus 6.73 ± 1.14 MPa, UTS 5.26 ± 2.25 MPa, elongation 79 ± 22%, thickness 19.06 ± 3.36 µm. Ex vivo cardiac studies (rat LV slices 300 µm; optical mapping with di-4-ANEPPS/blebbistatin, pacing 400 bpm) showed the conductive patch immediately decreases conduction velocity in healthy rat hearts (66.3→47.8 cm/s, p<0.02, n=5) and increases CV in infarcted hearts (24.3→30.1 cm/s, p<0.05, n=5), while non-conductive patches had no effect. Sutureless photoadhesion used Rose Bengal/chitosan + 532 nm laser (170 ± 8 mW, 180 ± 9 s, 110 J/cm²). In vivo rat LAD ligation model showed no proarrhythmogenic activity and a small significant increase in EF/FS. UV-Vis peaks at 320 nm (π-π*), 420 nm (emeraldine salt), 500–900 nm (polaron, shifts to 650 nm at day 10), confirming stable doped state.
authors: Damia Mawad, Camille Marijon, Filippo Perbellini, J. Tonkin, A. R. Lacour, C. M. Terracciano, S. E. Harding, M. M. Stevens, J. Felicario, A. Lacour, G. W. N., M. M. M., D. P., D. J. C., A. P. M., J. J. G., S. O. B. (Hardy J. Justin as noted editor note on retrieved page; principal contributors include Mawad D., Stevens M.M.)

title: A conducting polymer with enhanced electronic stability applied in cardiac models — Methods & Technical Details
text: Fabrication: Chitosan film (1.6 mL, 1.8 wt% acetic acid solution, dried 2 weeks); PANI grown by drop-casting 77 µL Solution 1 (phytic acid 2.30 mL + aniline 1.15 mL + DI H₂O 5 mL) + 23 µL Solution 2 (APS 1.42 g + DI H₂O 5 mL), polymerized 3 h, rinsed, peeled. Electronic characterization: CV (CHI660D, 25 mV/s in 0.1 M PBS pH 7.4), two-probe I-V for surface resistivity, UV-Vis (300–900 nm; peaks: π-π* 320 nm, polaron/emeraldine 420 nm, polaron band 500–900 nm), XPS (N 1s: =N− 6%, −NH 77%, −NH⁺ 12%, =NH⁺ 6% at day 0; polaron blue-shift to 650 nm at day 10). Mechanical: Young's modulus 6.73 ± 1.14 MPa, UTS 5.26 ± 2.25 MPa, elongation 79 ± 22%, contact angle 62.1 ± 1.4°. Ex vivo: cardiac slices (300 µm, rat LV, MEA1060); whole Langendorff hearts (optical mapping with di-4-ANEPPS + blebbistatin; pacing at 400 bpm). In vivo: Sprague-Dawley rats 250–350 g, LAD ligation for MI, photoadhesion with Rose Bengal/chitosan + 532 nm (170 ± 8 mW, 110 J/cm²), echocardiography (Vevo 2100), ECG (adrenaline 0.5 mg/kg IP). Materials: chitosan (medium MW, 85% deacetylation), aniline, phytic acid (50 wt%), APS, Rose Bengal (0.1 wt%), PCL, CSA, chloroform, acetic acid.

title: A conducting polymer with enhanced electronic stability applied in cardiac models — Key Findings
text: 1. Key innovation: immobilizing phytic acid dopant in PANI/chitosan scaffold prevents electric deterioration—patch retains electroactivity for >2 weeks in physiological medium. 2. Surface resistivity: dry 7.93 ± 2.64 kΩ/sq; after 14 days PBS: 35.85 ± 9.40 kΩ/sq (vs. PCL-PANI which loses conductivity within 24 h). 3. Conductivity (dry): 0.162 ± 0.043 S/cm. 4. XPS: ~12% protonated N species retained after 14 days (vs. ~6% for PCL-PANI after 7 days). 5. Mechanical properties: Young's modulus 6.73 ± 1.14 MPa; UTS 5.26 ± 2.25 MPa; elongation at break 79 ± 22%; thickness 19.06 ± 3.36 µm. 6. Ex vivo cardiac: conductive patch decreased CV in healthy hearts (66.3→47.8 cm/s, p<0.02) and increased CV in infarcted hearts (24.3→30.1 cm/s, p<0.05); non-conductive patch had no effect. 7. Sutureless photoadhesion: Rose Bengal + 532 nm laser (170 ± 8 mW, 180 ± 9 s, 110 J/cm²). 8. In vivo: no proarrhythmogenic activity; small significant increase in EF and FS vs. sham. 9. Fabrication: chitosan film (1.8 wt% in 2% acetic acid) + drop-cast aniline/phytic acid/APS polymerized 3 h.

title: An Electroactive Oligo-EDOT Platform for Neural Tissue Engineering
text: Ritzau-Reid et al. (2020, Adv. Funct. Mater. 30(42):2003710, PMC7610826) developed oligoEDOT-PCL ABA block co-polymers as a novel electroactive, processable, and biodegradable platform for neural tissue engineering. Using a novel end-capping synthetic strategy (iterative glyoxylation/bromination/Pd-catalyzed arylation), defined-length oligoEDOT constructs (n=2–5) served as macroinitiators for ROP of poly(caprolactone), yielding co-polymers of ≈25 kDa total MW (two PCL ≈12 kDa flanking oligoEDOT ≈1 kDa). Optical band gaps span 2.48 eV (diEDOT-PCL) to 2.03 eV (pentaEDOT-PCL) with max absorbance at 450–500 nm. TetraEDOT-PCL shows stable redox activity (anodic 0.8–1.1 V; cathodic 0.4–1 V at 0.1 V/s). Solution electrospinning (50:50 blend, 20 w/v% CHCl₃/MeOH, 12 kV) produced ≈400 nm fibers; melt electrospinning writing yielded 5×5 cm 3D lattices with 5 µm fibers and 100 µm grid spacing (10 layers). Human iPSC-derived neural stem cells (Epi-hiPSC) seeded on tetraEDOT-PCL films showed biocompatibility comparable to PCL and glass controls. Electrical stimulation (1 ms pulses, 600 mV, 1 Hz, 24 h) increased mean NSC neurite length from 111.4 ± 8.7 µm (unstimulated) to 142.1 ± 10.4 µm (stimulated) and enhanced branching, while PCL controls showed a reduction (82.4→60.5 µm), demonstrating the specific role of the oligoEDOT block in stimulation-enhanced neurite outgrowth.
authors: Kaja I. Ritzau-Reid, Christopher D. Spicer, Amy Gelmi, Christopher L. Grigsby, James F. Ponder Jr., Victoria Bemmer, Adam Creamer, Ramon Vilar, Andrea Serio, Molly M. Stevens

title: An Electroactive Oligo-EDOT Platform for Neural Tissue Engineering — Methods & Technical Details
text: Synthesis: iterative thiophene glyoxylation/bromination/chain extension/Pd-catalyzed direct arylation; Boc deprotection; ring-opening polymerization (ROP) of ε-caprolactone using amino-oligoEDOT macroinitiators. Film preparation: spin coating (6 w/v% CHCl₃, 1,500 rpm, 20 s) on glass/ITO. AFM: tetraEDOT-PCL fibrils ≈40 nm; PCL spherules 1.5–2 µm. UV-Vis: E_g = 1242/λ_onset. CV: Autolab PGSTAT101, 3-electrode (Ag/AgCl ref, Pt counter, glassy C working), −0.45 to 1.20 V, 100 mV/s. Electrospinning: 20 w/v% 9:1 CHCl₃:MeOH, 12 kV, 20 µL/min, 15 cm. MEW: 50:50 blend, 70–80°C, 10 kV, 10 mm distance, 1.0 Bar, 1,500 mm/min, 10 layers. Cell culture: human iPSC-derived NSCs (Epi-hiPSC line); seeding density 10×10⁴/well; FGF2 (20 ng/mL) for proliferation; basal medium for differentiation. Stimulation: eDaq EA163 potentiostat, Pt counter, 1 ms pulses, 600 mV, 1 Hz, 24 h. Hydrogels: 5% w/w with 8-arm PEG macromers. Staining: βIII-tubulin (neurons), nestin (progenitors/stem cells), Ki-67 (proliferation), DAPI. Statistics: one-way ANOVA with Tukey's; *p<0.05, **p<0.001; n=2–4 biological replicates, min 12–15 images/condition.

title: An Electroactive Oligo-EDOT Platform for Neural Tissue Engineering — Key Findings
text: 1. Novel end-capping strategy to synthesize defined-length oligoEDOT constructs (n=2–5 chain lengths) via iterative thiophene glyoxylation, bromination, chain extension, and Pd-catalyzed direct arylation. 2. OligoEDOT-PCL ABA block co-polymers (total MW ≈25 kDa: two PCL ≈12 kDa flanking a central oligoEDOT ≈1 kDa core) are electroactive, processable, and biocompatible. 3. Optical band gaps: 2.48 eV (diEDOT-PCL) to 2.03 eV (pentaEDOT-PCL); red-shifted max absorbance 450–500 nm. 4. TetraEDOT-PCL shows stable redox activity (CV anodic 0.8–1.1 V, cathodic 0.4–1 V; 0.1 V/s, TBAPF6). 5. Solution electrospinning (50:50 tetraEDOT-PCL:high MW PCL, 20 w/v% in 9:1 CHCl₃:MeOH, 12 kV, 20 µL/min, 15 cm) produces ≈400 nm diameter fibers. 6. Melt electrospinning writing (MEW) produced 5×5 cm 3D lattices with 5 µm fiber diameter and 100 µm grid spacing (10 layers). 7. iPSC-derived NSC culture: biocompatible substrate, no significant cytotoxicity vs. PCL or glass controls. 8. Electrical stimulation (1 ms pulses, 600 mV, 1 Hz, 24 h) on tetraEDOT-PCL films: mean NSC neurite length 142.1 ± 10.4 µm (stimulated) vs. 111.4 ± 8.7 µm (unstimulated); also increased branching. PCL control showed decrease in neurite length under stimulation (82.4→60.5 µm).

title: Microliter Scale Synthesis of Luciferase-Encapsulated Polymersomes as Artificial Organelles for Optogenetic Modulation of Cardiomyocyte Beating
text: Kim and Yeow et al. (2022, Adv. Sci. 9:2200239, PMID 35901502) demonstrated microliter-scale (10 µL) synthesis of Gaussia luciferase (GLuc)-encapsulated polymersomes (PSomes) as artificial organelles (AOs) for optogenetic cardiomyocyte modulation. PSomes were produced by photoiniferter RAFT-PISA (PEG-CDTPA + HPMA; 405 nm, 10 mW/cm², 3 h; mineral oil overlay for deoxygenation-free synthesis), yielding 231 ± 41 nm diameter particles (PDI 0.13 ± 0.02) with 77 ± 3% HPMA conversion and 29 ± 4 GLuc proteins/PSome (FCS, n=3) at 7.4 ± 1.2% encapsulation efficiency. The polymersome membrane conferred exceptional enzymatic resistance: 88.0 ± 5.7% luminescence retention after porcine elastase vs. 14.6% for free GLuc; 32.1 ± 12.8% after 1 month at 37°C vs. 2.6% free GLuc. GLuc/PSomes were internalized into optogenetically engineered iPSC-CMs (WTC-11, >85% cTnT+; ChR2-EYFP) via clathrin-mediated endocytosis and macropinocytosis (34 ± 6% and 30 ± 5% inhibition by CPZ and EIPA; >95% double-positive uptake at 24 h). Upon coelenterazine (CTZ) addition, intracellular bioluminescence (λ=480 nm) activated ChR2, inducing a 37 ± 7% increase in beating frequency at day 0 and 41 ± 16% at day 7 (both p<0.001 vs. free GLuc), demonstrating stable long-term AO function after 7 days in culture. This represents the first AO system producing a cell-mediated mechanical response.
authors: Hyemin Kim, Jonathan Yeow, Adrian Najer, Worrapong Kit-Anan, Richard Wang, Omar Rifaie-Graham, Chalaisorn Thanapongpibul, Molly M. Stevens

title: Microliter Scale Synthesis of Luciferase-Encapsulated Polymersomes as Artificial Organelles for Optogenetic Modulation of Cardiomyocyte Beating — Methods & Technical Details
text: Synthesis: Photoiniferter-initiated RAFT polymerization (PISA) in 10 µL (also 100 µL scale-up); PEG-CDTPA chain extension with HPMA; 405 nm, 10 mW/cm², 3 h; deoxygenation-free ('polymerizing-through' oxygen + mineral oil overlay 2.5 µL); 1536-well plate (triplicate). Polymers: PEG-CDTPA, HPMA, AzHPMA, RhoBMA. Characterization: ¹H NMR (conversion), GPC (MW), DLS (size/PDI), cryo-TEM (morphology), FCS/FCCS (cargo association, 29 ± 4 GLuc/PSome), Micro BCA (encapsulation efficiency post-lysis). GLuc concentration: 1 mg/mL in synthesis; working conc: 100–200 ng/mL. iPSC-CMs: WTC-11 line, >85% cTnT+ purity (flow cytometry); ChR2-EYFP lentiviral transduction; chemically defined differentiation protocol. Optogenetic stimulation: 470 nm LED (1 Hz, 100 ms pulse or continuous); calcium transients with Rhod-4 AM; beating quantified with MYOCYTER ImageJ plugin. Statistics: ANOVA/Tukey; mean ± SD, ≥3 replicates; p<0.001 significance threshold.

title: Microliter Scale Synthesis of Luciferase-Encapsulated Polymersomes as Artificial Organelles for Optogenetic Modulation of Cardiomyocyte Beating — Key Findings
text: 1. First demonstration of polymersome-based artificial organelles (AOs) synthesized at 10 µL (microliter) scale via photoiniferter RAFT-PISA, enabling use of cost-prohibitive enzymes (GLuc >$1,000/mg). 2. HPMA conversion: 77 ± 3% (n=3). 3. PSome hydrodynamic diameter: 231 ± 41 nm, PDI 0.13 ± 0.02. 4. GLuc loading: 29 ± 4 proteins/PSome (FCS); encapsulation efficiency 7.4 ± 1.2% (Micro BCA). 5. Luminescence emission at λ=480 nm. 6. Stability: GLuc/PSomes retain 32.1 ± 12.8% luminescence after 1 month at 37°C vs. only 2.6 ± 0.2% for free GLuc. 7. Proteolytic resistance: 88.0 ± 5.7% activity retained after porcine elastase (0.5 µg/mL, 24 h) vs. 14.6% free GLuc. 8. Uptake: >95% of iPSC-CMs double positive for GLuc-Cy5 and PSome-RhoB at 24 h (flow cytometry); clathrin-mediated pathway and macropinocytosis involved (34 ± 6% and 30 ± 5% inhibition by CPZ and EIPA). 9. Beating frequency increase: 37 ± 7% at day 0 and 41 ± 16% at day 7 post-uptake, upon CTZ addition (p<0.001 vs. free GLuc). 10. Long-term stability: >90% of iPSC-CMs remain double-positive after 7 days; GLuc/PSomes retain 42.3 ± 8.3% activity vs. 3.6 ± 0.4% for free GLuc.

title: Single Particle Automated Raman Trapping Analysis
text: Penders et al. (2018, Nat. Commun. DOI: 10.1038/s41467-018-06397-6, PMID 30323298) developed SPARTA, a platform combining a confocal Raman spectrometer (WITec alpha300R+, 785 nm laser, 85 mW, 63×/1.0 NA water objective, 3 cm⁻¹ resolution) with automated optical trapping via custom MATLAB scripts. Three operational modes were demonstrated. Mode I (composition/functionalisation): resolved 50:50 v/v DPPC/d-DPPC liposome mixtures using the C-D Raman peak at 2,105 cm⁻¹ (n=828 particles), achieving bimodal separation with Gaussian mixture analysis; distinguished ABA vs. ABA-heparin polymersomes by PCA/Ward's (97.9% PLSDA specificity, n=263). Mode II (sizing): sodium perchlorate (50 mM) acts as solution marker, with perchlorate signal (938 cm⁻¹) decreasing as particles enter the confocal volume; calibration curve R²=0.99 for 50/100/200 nm PS particles, with SPARTA yielding lower PDI than DLS. Mode III (reaction monitoring): tracked CuAAC click chemistry on PS nanoparticles (alkyne 2,129 cm⁻¹ → triazole 1,331 cm⁻¹; completion ~8 min population-level, ~2 min single-particle hold). Sequential surface functionalisations of 200 nm amine-PS particles were tracked step-by-step. Sample requirements: 200 µL at 1×10¹⁰–10¹² particles/mL. No photodamage observed in 5-min trapped liposomes. SPARTA enables label-free, concurrent size and composition characterization of individual sub-diffraction-limit nanoparticles.
authors: Jelle Penders, Isaac J. Pence, Conor C. Horgan, Mads S. Bergholt, Christopher S. Wood, Adrian Najer, Ulrike Kauscher, Anika Nagelkerke, Molly M. Stevens

title: Single Particle Automated Raman Trapping Analysis — Methods & Technical Details
text: Instrument: confocal Raman (alpha300R+, WITec); 785 nm laser (Toptica XTRA II, 85 mW at sample); 63×/1.0 NA water immersion objective (Zeiss); 100 µm fibre, 600 grooves/mm grating (UHTS 300, WITec); CCD (iDus DU401-DD, Andor; 3 cm⁻¹ resolution). Automation: custom MATLAB scripts (threshold-based trap detection). Iteration parameters: 1 s iteration acquisition, 10 s high-SNR acquisition, 0.5–1 s laser disable time; max 10 iterations. Data processing: truncate 350–1,825 cm⁻¹ (or 606–2,254 cm⁻¹ for alkynes); cosmic removal (peak/2nd derivative); Whittaker baseline (λ=100,000); Savitzky-Golay smooth (3 pts, 1st order); area normalization (except sizing). Key spectral peaks: C-D (d-DPPC) 2,105 cm⁻¹; S-H bend 936 cm⁻¹; perchlorate (sizing) 938 cm⁻¹; alkyne 2,129 cm⁻¹; azide 2,116 cm⁻¹; triazole 1,331 cm⁻¹; PDMS 490/709 cm⁻¹; CH₂ 1,450 cm⁻¹; heparin ~930/1,070 cm⁻¹. Liposomes: DPPC/cholesterol 85:15 mol%; extruded 31× through 200 nm membrane at 60°C. Polymersomes: ABA (PMOXA-b-PDMS-b-PMOXA, Mn 0.5-4.8-0.5 kDa) + ABA-heparin (25 wt% PDMS-b-heparin); extruded, SEC-purified. PS particles: amine/carboxyl 200 nm (Polysciences). CuAAC reagents: 100 mM CuSO₄, sodium ascorbate, azidoacetic acid.

title: Single Particle Automated Raman Trapping Analysis — Key Findings
text: 1. SPARTA enables automated high-throughput analysis of hundreds of individual nanoparticles (vs. tens in manual systems) combining optical trapping with confocal Raman spectroscopy. 2. Three modes: (I) composition/functionalisation analysis; (II) solution marker-mediated sizing; (III) on-line dynamic reaction monitoring. 3. Mode I: Resolved 50:50 v/v% DPPC/d-DPPC liposome mixture (C-D peak at 2,105 cm⁻¹; bimodal histogram n=828 particles): 44% DPPC (n=649), 56% d-DPPC (n=340). ABA vs. ABA-heparin polymersomes resolved by PCA/Ward's clustering (49% ABA, 47% ABA-heparin, 4% unclassified; n=263) and PLSDA (97.9% specificity). 4. Mode II: Particle sizing with 50 mM sodium perchlorate; perchlorate ratio linear vs. log volume (R²=0.99) for 50/100/200 nm PS particles; SPARTA PDI lower than DLS. DLS reference sizes: 50 nm (53.6 ± 13.1 nm), 100 nm (83.0 ± 15.7 nm), 200 nm (162.8 ± 41.5 nm). 5. Mode III: CuAAC click reaction on alkyne-PS nanoparticles (alkyne: 2,129 cm⁻¹; azide: 2,116 cm⁻¹; triazole: 1,331 cm⁻¹); completion ~8 min (population) or ~2 min (single particle hold). 6. Sequential functionalisation of 200 nm amine-PS particles tracked: sulfhydryl (S-H at 936 cm⁻¹), disulfide, CYY tripeptide (tyrosine peaks). 7. No photodamage in DPPC liposomes held 5 min (Supplementary Fig. 1). 8. Sample requirements: 200 µL, 1×10¹⁰–10¹² particles/mL.

title: Hierarchically Designed Bone Scaffolds: From Internal Cues to External Stimuli (Note: this is a review from Huazhong/Rice, cited in Stevens corpus context; Stevens group primary bone mineral work includes Individual response variations in scaffold-guided bone regeneration, Biomaterials 2018, PMC6345626)
text: Du et al. (2019, Biomaterials 218:119334, PMC6663598) reviewed hierarchically designed bone scaffolds across four levels. At the molecular level, element-doped HA variants are discussed: Si-HA (0.8 wt% optimal) enhances osteoblast attachment/differentiation and inhibits osteoclasts; Se-HA (3% Se/P) selectively induces osteosarcoma apoptosis; Sr-HA (3–7%, 10 mol%) increases osteogenesis/angiogenesis; Zn-HA and Cu/Co-HA provide anti-inflammatory/antibacterial and angiogenic benefits. At the nanoscale, molecular template biomineralization (collagen, silk fibroin, bi-templates) produces intrafibrillar HA with controlled crystal size and orientation; bi-template ALB-SF induces nanospheres with faster MSC uptake and superior osteogenic differentiation. At the micro/nanoscale, 40–50 µm grooves, 120 nm/100 nm deep nanopits, and 11–85 nm nano-islands are optimal; 80–90% porosity with >300 µm pores is required for vascularization; 3D printing enables graded pore structures. At the macroscale, cyclic mechanical compression, piezoelectric scaffolds (P(VDF-TrFE)/BaTiO₃), ES (200 µA; 200–400 mV/cm, 4 h/day, 21 days), SMF/EMF, and photothermal stimuli (808 nm NIR—GO-TCP: >90% osteosarcoma death, C₃N₄: 91% bone recovery at 4 weeks) all regulate osteogenesis. Note: this review is not directly from the Stevens Group (Huazhong/Rice authors); Stevens Group primary bone scaffold papers include work on scaffold stiffness and osteogenic response (PMC6345626).
authors: Yingying Du, Jason L. Guo, Jianglin Wang, Antonios G. Mikos, Shengmin Zhang

title: Hierarchically Designed Bone Scaffolds: From Internal Cues to External Stimuli (Note: this is a review from Huazhong/Rice, cited in Stevens corpus context; Stevens group primary bone mineral work includes Individual response variations in scaffold-guided bone regeneration, Biomaterials 2018, PMC6345626) — Methods & Technical Details
text: Material systems reviewed: HA, β-TCP, bioactive glass, doped HA (CHA, FHA, Si-HA, Zn-HA, Mg-HA, Sr-HA, Se-HA, Fe-HA, Co-HA, Cu-HA); polymers: COL I, SF, PLA, PLGA, PCL, PVDF, PPY, PANI, PEDOT, chitosan, gelatin, hyaluronic acid; inorganic: MNPs (Fe₃O₄, Fe₂O₃), BaTiO₃, graphene, CNTs, GO. Fabrication methods: electrospinning, 3D printing (FDM, SLA, SLS), phase separation, sol-gel (for mesoporous bioactive glass). Physical stimuli: cyclic mechanical compression, electrical stimulation (ES), static magnetic field (SMF), electromagnetic field (EMF), near-infrared laser (NIR, 808 nm). Key measurements: Si-HA optimal 0.8 wt%; Sr-HA 3.22 mol% optimal in vitro; groove 40–50 µm; nanopits 120 nm dia/100 nm deep; porosity 80–90%; >300 µm for capillaries; hydrogel moduli 10–300 kPa; ES 200 µA/200–400 mV/cm; NIR 808 nm photothermal; 91% bone recovery at 4 weeks.

title: Hierarchically Designed Bone Scaffolds: From Internal Cues to External Stimuli (Note: this is a review from Huazhong/Rice, cited in Stevens corpus context; Stevens group primary bone mineral work includes Individual response variations in scaffold-guided bone regeneration, Biomaterials 2018, PMC6345626) — Key Findings
text: 1. Hierarchical bone scaffold design spans: (1) molecular—element-doped hydroxyapatite (HA); (2) nanoscale—template-induced biomineralization; (3) micro/nanoscale—scaffold architecture; (4) macroscale—external physical stimuli. 2. Optimal Si substitution in HA: 0.8 wt% enhances osteoblast attachment/differentiation, inhibits osteoclasts, increases in vivo bone ingrowth. 3. Se-HA: 3% Se/P ratio induces osteosarcoma apoptosis while supporting BMSCs. 4. Sr-HA: 3–7% Sr, 10 mol%, increases osteogenesis/angiogenesis. 5. Optimal scaffold porosity: 80–90%, >300 µm pore size for vascularization. 6. Groove dimensions: 40–50 µm optimal for osteogenesis. 7. Nano-islands: 11–85 nm height, 100–200 nm wide—enhance osteoblast adhesion, spreading, ALP, mineralization. 8. Piezoelectric scaffolds (P(VDF-TrFE)+BaTiO₃) restore electric microenvironment, enhance rat calvarial regeneration. 9. Photothermal (GO-TCP, 808 nm NIR): >90% osteosarcoma cell death, 91% bone recovery in 4 weeks. 10. Electrical stimulation: 200 µA, 200–400 mV/cm, 4 h/day, 21 days.

title: Stevens Group Proposed Project 2: AI-Driven Autonomous Discovery of Advanced Biomaterials
text: This proposal introduces an autonomous, AI-driven framework to accelerate the discovery and design of advanced biomaterials. Currently, developing complex treatments like auxetic conductive cardiac patches or injectable hydrogels for immunomodulation requires highly fragmented, labor-intensive iteration. By utilizing DVNC.ai, this workflow automates the extraction of critical design parameters from vast scientific literature, tracking complex variables like mechanical stiffness, polymer conductivity, and bioactive factor release profiles. The AI then synthesizes these cross-disciplinary insights to propose novel composite formulations, finding synergistic overlaps. For instance, the model could theoretically design a material combining the electrical properties of conductive polymers with the targeted, localized delivery of angiogenic or anti-inflammatory factors like bFGF or TIMP-3 to prevent adverse tissue remodeling. The target application is auxetic cardiac patches that combine mechanical strain-matching via negative Poisson ratio geometry, electrical conductivity via PEDOT or polyaniline doping, immunomodulatory factor release including TIMP-3 for MMP inhibition and bFGF for angiogenesis, and optogenetic control via polymersome-encapsulated bioluminescent proteins.

title: Stevens Group Proposed Project 1: Self-Extending RamanSPy Prompt-Driven Method Generation
text: Building upon recent advances in Raman spectroscopy analysis and machine learning, this proposal introduces a prompt-driven system based on DVNC.ai platform that enables the automatic extension of the RamanSPy framework through natural language instructions. RamanSPy provides a modular and extensible Python ecosystem for Raman spectroscopy data analysis. Recent research has demonstrated the effectiveness of physics-constrained deep learning models, such as autoencoders, for hyperspectral Raman unmixing. DVNC interprets natural language prompts, generates compatible Python code following RamanSPy architectural conventions, integrates the generated module into the existing library structure, and performs automated validation through unit tests and spectral datasets. The system can iteratively refine the generated implementation based on execution feedback, ensuring correctness and compatibility with RamanSPy pipeline. This framework transforms RamanSPy into a self-extensible spectroscopy platform.
